title: Cat Foul-Smelling Dark Ear Discharge: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn what dark, smelly ear discharge in cats means, why it is a medium-priority concern, and how photos help your vet provide the right treatment.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dark, foul-smelling ear discharge in cats usually indicates ear mites or an infection and requires a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours. Seek immediate care if your cat exhibits a head tilt, loss of balance, or extreme lethargy.

What causes my cat to have dark, foul-smelling ear discharge?

  • This symptom is most commonly associated with ear mites, yeast infections, or bacterial overgrowth.
  • The dark material is typically a combination of earwax, blood, and inflammatory debris.
  • A strong, foul odor usually points toward an active infection that requires professional medical intervention.

Is my cat’s ear discharge a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level is Medium; you should seek an appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • While not usually a life-threatening ER visit, it is a painful condition that needs prompt attention.
  • Seek immediate care if your cat shows a head tilt, loss of balance, or extreme lethargy, as these can indicate the infection has reached the inner ear.

How does taking a photo of the ear help the vet triage my cat?

  • A high-quality photo allows the triage team to see the color and texture of the discharge.
  • It helps distinguish between the 'coffee-ground' appearance of mites and the moist, yellow-brown discharge of yeast.
  • Visuals of swelling or redness help the team assess your cat's pain level and prioritize your appointment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, foul-smelling, dark ear discharge can indicate otitis externa or media/interna. Clinical signs may include head shaking, rubbing or scratching at the affected ear, and head tilting. Self-trauma can lead to aural hematoma. Otitis media may present with facial nerve paralysis (ear droop, lip droop, ptosis, collapse of the nostril) or Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, enophthalmos, protrusion of the nictitating membrane). Diagnosis involves otic cytology, skin scrapings, cleansing, and topical antimicrobial/anti-inflammatory therapy. Management of pain and pruritus should be included in the initial treatment, potentially with tramadol. Glucocorticoids can be beneficial in decreasing swelling of the ear canal.

Chapter: Dermatology, ENT

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1834)

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter ear drops?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to use over-the-counter drops without a diagnosis. If the eardrum is ruptured, certain medications can cause permanent deafness or neurological issues.

faqs.question: Should I clean the ear before the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: No, please avoid cleaning the ear before your visit. The veterinarian needs to take a sample of the discharge to look at under a microscope to identify exactly what is causing the infection.

faqs.question: Are ear infections painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear infections are significantly painful. Cats are experts at hiding discomfort, but scratching at the ear or shaking the head are clear signs of distress.
